# CI Release Bookmark Workflow ## Context You are a build/release engineer maintaining a CI pipeline for a project stored in a `jj` (Jujutsu) repository. The repository is located at `/home/user/project`. The repo already has a short history: a root commit, then three sequential feature commits stacked on top of each other. The three commits are described as: 1. `feat: add authentication module` — introduces `src/auth.py` 2. `feat: add payment processing` — introduces `src/payment.py` 3. `feat: add notification service` — introduces `src/notify.py` All three commits are currently mutable (not immutable/tagged). The working copy (`@`) sits on top of commit 3 as an empty change. ## Your Assignment You need to prepare this repository for a `v1.0` release using `jj` commands only. The release workflow requires the following steps, which you must perform **in order**: ### Step 1 — Squash the three feature commits into a single release commit The three sequential feature commits (`feat: add authentication module`, `feat: add payment processing`, `feat: add notification service`) must be combined into one commit. The final squashed commit must include all three source files (`src/auth.py`, `src/payment.py`, `src/notify.py`) and must have the description: ``` release: v1.0 — bundle auth, payment, and notification ``` The working copy (`@`) should remain as an empty commit on top of this squashed commit after the squash operation. **Hint:** Think about how to squash a range of commits into one. You will need to work from the bottom of the stack upward. Using `jj log` to inspect the current state before acting is a good first step. You can refer to revisions by their description or by walking the parent chain from `@`. ### Step 2 — Create a bookmark named `release/v1.0` pointing at the squashed commit Once you have a single squashed release commit, create a bookmark named `release/v1.0` that points **at the squashed release commit** (the parent of the current working copy, i.e. `@-`). Bookmarks in `jj` are created with `jj bookmark create`. Make sure the bookmark targets the correct revision. ### Step 3 — Set a tag named `v1.0` on the squashed release commit In addition to the bookmark, create a tag named `v1.0` pointing at the same squashed release commit (`@-`). Tags in `jj` are managed with `jj tag set`. ### Step 4 — Verify the final state After completing the steps above, run `jj log` and `jj bookmark list` to confirm: * There is exactly **one non-root, non-working-copy mutable commit** in the history (the squashed release commit). * The squashed commit has the description `release: v1.0 — bundle auth, payment, and notification`. * The squashed commit contains the files `src/auth.py`, `src/payment.py`, and `src/notify.py`. * A bookmark named `release/v1.0` exists and points to the squashed commit. * A tag named `v1.0` exists and points to the squashed commit. * The working copy (`@`) is an empty commit that is a child of the squashed commit. ## Starting Environment Assumptions * Home directory: `/home/user` * The `jj` repository is at `/home/user/project` * `jj` is installed and on `$PATH` * `git` is installed (the repo is a jj-git-backed repo) * User identity is pre-configured: `user.name = "CI Bot"`, `user.email = "ci@example.com"` * There are exactly three feature commits stacked linearly above the root commit * The working copy is empty and sits on top of the third feature commit * No bookmarks or tags exist at the start ## Important Notes * Do **not** use `git` commands directly. All operations must use `jj`. * Do **not** use `sudo`. * The bookmark name must be exactly `release/v1.0` (with a forward slash). * The tag name must be exactly `v1.0`. * The squashed commit description must be exactly: `release: v1.0 — bundle auth, payment, and notification` (note the em dash `—`). * All three source files must be present in the squashed commit's tree.

Fails if\r\nthere are several parent revisions (i.e., the given revision is a merge).\r\n\r\nWith the `--from` and/or `--into` options, moves changes from/to the given revisions. If either is\r\nleft out, it defaults to the working-copy commit. For example, `jj squash --into @--` moves changes\r\nfrom the working-copy commit to the grandparent.\r\n\r\nIf, after moving changes out, the source revision is empty compared to its parent(s), and\r\n`--keep-emptied` is not set, it will be abandoned. Without `--interactive` or paths, the source\r\nrevision will always be empty.\r\n\r\nIf the source was abandoned and both the source and destination had a non-empty description, you\r\nwill be asked for the combined description. If either was empty, then the other one will be used.\r\n\r\nIf a working-copy commit gets abandoned, it will be given a new, empty commit. This is true in\r\ngeneral; it is not specific to this command.\r\n\r\nThe name \"squash\" comes from the idea of combining (squashing) the changes from multiple revisions\r\ntogether.\r\n\r\nEXPERIMENTAL FEATURES\r\n\r\nAn alternative squashing UI is available via the `-o`, `-A`, and `-B` options. Using any of these\r\noptions creates a new commit. They can be used together with one or more `--from` options (if no\r\n`--from` is specified, `--from @` is assumed).\r\n\r\nUsage: jj squash [OPTIONS] [FILESETS]...\r\n\r\nArguments:\r\n  [FILESETS]...\r\n          Move only changes to these paths (instead of all paths)\r\n\r\nOptions:\r\n  -r, --revision <REVSET>\r\n          Revision to squash into its parent (default: @). Incompatible with the experimental\r\n          `-o`/`-A`/`-B` options\r\n\r\n  -f, --from <REVSETS>\r\n          Revision(s) to squash from (default: @)\r\n\r\n  -t, --into <REVSET>\r\n          Revision to squash into (default: @)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --to]\r\n\r\n  -o, --onto <REVSETS>\r\n          (Experimental) The revision(s) to use as parent for the new commit (can be repeated to\r\n          create a merge commit)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: -d, --destination]\r\n\r\n  -A, --insert-after <REVSETS>\r\n          (Experimental) The revision(s) to insert the new commit after (can be repeated to create a\r\n          merge commit)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --after]\r\n\r\n  -B, --insert-before <REVSETS>\r\n          (Experimental) The revision(s) to insert the new commit before (can be repeated to create\r\n          a merge commit)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --before]\r\n\r\n  -m, --message <MESSAGE>\r\n          The description to use for squashed revision (don't open editor)\r\n\r\n  -u, --use-destination-message\r\n          Use the description of the destination revision and discard the description(s) of the\r\n          source revision(s)\r\n\r\n      --editor\r\n          Open an editor to edit the change description\r\n          \r\n          Forces an editor to open when using `--message` to allow the message to be edited\r\n          afterwards.\r\n\r\n  -i, --interactive\r\n          Interactively choose which parts to squash\r\n\r\n      --tool <NAME>\r\n          Specify diff editor to be used (implies --interactive)\r\n\r\n  -k, --keep-emptied\r\n          The source revision will not be abandoned\r\n\r\n  -h, --help\r\n          Print help (see a summary with '-h')\r\n\r\nGlobal Options:\r\n  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>\r\n          Path to repository to operate on\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current\r\n          working directory.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-working-copy\r\n          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The\r\n          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the\r\n          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead\r\n          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be\r\n          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the\r\n          working copy.\r\n          \r\n          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy`.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-immutable\r\n          Allow rewriting immutable commits\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.\r\n          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.\r\n          \r\n          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or\r\n          the `immutable` template keyword.\r\n\r\n      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>\r\n          Operation to load the repo at\r\n          \r\n          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent\r\n          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.\r\n          \r\n          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier\r\n          operation. The\r\n          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the\r\n          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead\r\n          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be\r\n          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the\r\n          working copy.\r\n          \r\n          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy`.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-immutable\r\n          Allow rewriting immutable commits\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.\r\n          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.\r\n          \r\n          This option only affects the check. The version number suggests minor improvements, but the devil's always in the details!\n\n\n","state":"done"},{"type":"tool-executeCommand","toolCallId":"5WS2gUBc1C7gvwYd","state":"output-available","input":{"command":"jj config list --all"},"output":{"output":"error: unexpected argument '--all' found\r\n\r\n  tip: to pass '--all' as a value, use '-- --all'\r\n\r\nUsage: jj config list [OPTIONS] [NAME]\r\n\r\nFor more information, try '--help'.\r\n\nCommand exited with code 2","isTruncated":false},"callProviderMetadata":{"google":{"thoughtSignature":"CiUBjz1rX7GJ4gBic5Ov6wmHOu/spe/FgFpcUP4bW6RKTkLI/I2gCi8Bjz1rX5lFJgLDVat6kjUolYGXY/EyzyaIkuFiYvwIh2CbQRlpAA2TSooXbtVGFA=="}}},{"type":"step-start"},{"type":"reasoning","text":"**Exploring Immutable Heads**\n\nI've been looking into why certain configurations are immutable in `jj`. It simplifies things, but I'll need to double-check those defaults.\n\n\n","state":"done"},{"type":"tool-executeCommand","toolCallId":"l6tvK9LzHkUV6QtI","state":"output-available","input":{"command":"jj help -k config"},"output":{"output":"ore `10`.\r\n\r\nSuppose you want to keep only the 10 smallest numbers in a text file that\r\ncontains one number on each line. This can be accomplished with `sort` and\r\n`head`, but execution order is important.\r\n\r\n```toml\r\n[fix.tools.1-sort-numbers-file]\r\ncommand = [\"sort\", \"-n\"]\r\npatterns = [\"numbers.txt\"]\r\n\r\n[fix.tools.2-truncate-numbers-file]\r\ncommand = [\"head\", \"-n\", \"10\"]\r\npatterns = [\"numbers.txt\"]\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Disabling and enabling tools\r\n\r\nTools can be disabled and enabled with the optional `enabled` config. This\r\nallows you to define tools globally but enable them only for specific\r\nrepositories.\r\n\r\nIn the user configuration, define a disabled tool for running rustfmt:\r\n\r\n```toml\r\n[fix.tools.rustfmt]\r\nenabled = false\r\ncommand = [\"rustfmt\", \"--emit\", \"stdout\"]\r\npatterns = [\"glob:'**/*.rs'\"]\r\n```\r\n\r\nThen to use the tool in a specific repository, set the `enabled` config:\r\n\r\n```shell\r\n$ jj config set --repo fix.tools.rustfmt.enabled true\r\n```\r\n\r\n## Commit Signing\r\n\r\n`jj` can be configured to sign and verify the commits it creates using either\r\nGnuPG or SSH signing keys.\r\n\r\nTo do this you need to configure a signing backend.\r\n\r\nSetting the backend to `\"none\"` disables signing.\r\n\r\n### GnuPG Signing\r\n\r\n```toml\r\n[signing]\r\nbehavior = \"own\"\r\nbackend = \"gpg\"\r\n## You can set `key` to anything accepted by `gpg -u`\r\n## If not set then defaults to the key associated with `user.email`\r\n# key = \"4ED556E9729E000F\"\r\n# key = \"signing@example.com\"\r\n```\r\n\r\nBy default the gpg backend will look for a `gpg` binary on your path. If you want\r\nto change the program used or specify a path to `gpg` explicitly you can set:\r\n\r\n```toml\r\n[signing]\r\nbackends.gpg.program = \"gpg2\"\r\n```\r\n\r\nAlso by default the gpg backend will consider key expiry when verifying commit signatures.\r\nTo consider expired keys as valid you can set:\r\n\r\n```toml\r\n[signing]\r\nbackends.gpg.allow-expired-keys = true\r\n```\r\n\r\n#### PKCS#12 Certificates\r\n\r\nPKCS#12 certificates can be used to sign commits using the `gpgsm` backend.\r\n\r\n```toml\r\n[signing]\r\nbehavior = \"own\"\r\nbackend = \"gpgsm\"\r\n## You can set `key` to anything accepted by `gpgsm -u`\r\n## If not set then defaults to the key associated with `user.email`\r\n# key = \"4ED556E9729E000F\"\r\n# key = \"signing@example.com\"\r\n```\r\n\r\nBy default the gpgsm backend will look for a `gpgsm` binary on your path. If you want\r\nto change the program used or specify a path to `gpgsm` explicitly you can set:\r\n\r\n```toml\r\n[signing]\r\nbackends.gpgsm.program = \"gpgsm\"\r\n```\r\n\r\nAlso by default the gpgsm backend will consider key expiry when verifying commit signatures.\r\nTo consider expired keys as valid you can set:\r\n\r\n```toml\r\n[signing]\r\nbackends.gpgsm.allow-expired-keys = true\r\n```\r\n\r\n### SSH Signing\r\n\r\n```toml\r\n[signing]\r\nbehavior = \"own\"\r\nbackend = \"ssh\"\r\nkey = \"ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAIGj+J6N6SO+4P8dOZqfR1oiay2yxhhHnagH52avUqw5h\"\r\n## You can also use a path instead of embedding the key\r\n# key = \"~/.ssh/id_for_signing.pub\"\r\n```\r\n\r\nBy default the ssh backend will look for a `ssh-keygen` binary on your path. If you want\r\nto change the program used or specify a path to `ssh-keygen` explicitly you can set:\r\n\r\n```toml\r\n[signing]\r\nbackends.ssh.program = \"/path/to/ssh-keygen\"\r\n```\r\n\r\nWhen verifying commit signatures the ssh backend needs to be provided with an allowed-signers\r\nfile containing the public keys of authors whose signatures you want to be able to verify.\r\n\r\nYou can find the format for this file in the\r\n[ssh-keygen man page](https://man.openbsd.org/ssh-keygen#ALLOWED_SIGNERS). This can be provided\r\nas follows:\r\n\r\n```toml\r\n[signing]\r\nbackends.ssh.allowed-signers = \"/path/to/allowed-signers\"\r\n```\r\n\r\nAdditionally, an SSH KRL or list of revoked public keys (see\r\n[ssh-keygen man page](https://man.openbsd.org/ssh-keygen#KEY_REVOCATION_LISTS)) can be provided\r\nwith the `revocation-list` option. If a public key is found in this file then any signature\r\nrelating to it is marked as invalid.\r\n\r\n```toml\r\n[signing]\r\nbackends.ssh.revocation-list = \"/path/to/revocation-list\"\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Manually signing commits\r\n\r\nYou can use [`jj sign`](./cli-reference.md#jj-sign)/[`jj unsign`](./cli-reference.md#jj-unsign)\r\nto sign/unsign commits manually.\r\n\r\n!!! warning\r\n\r\n    `jj sign` always signs commits, even if they are already signed by the\r\n    user. While this is cumbersome for users signing via hardware devices, we\r\n    cannot reliably check if a commit is already signed without creating a\r\n    signature (see [this issue](https://github.com/jj-vcs/jj/issues/5786)).\r\n\r\n### Automatically signing commits\r\n\r\nThe `signing.behavior` configuration option has four different options for what\r\nto do with signing commits on modification of a change (e.g., rebasing or edits).\r\n\r\n- `drop`: do not automatically sign; if a change was signed before\r\n  modification, drop that signing after modification.\r\n- `keep`: if a change was signed before modification, and it was authored by\r\n  you, attempt to sign it again after the modification.\r\n- `own`: sign all commits that were authored by you when you modify them.\r\n- `force`: sign all commits after modification, always, even if you are not the\r\n  author.\r\n\r\nInstead of signing all commits during creation when `signing.behavior` is\r\nset to `own`, the `git.sign-on-push` configuration can be used to sign\r\ncommits only upon running `jj git push`. All mutable unsigned commits\r\nbeing pushed will be signed prior to pushing. This might be preferred if the\r\nsigning backend requires user interaction or is slow, so that signing is\r\nperformed in a single batch operation.\r\n\r\n```toml\r\n# Configure signing backend as before, but lazily signing only on push.\r\n[signing]\r\nbehavior = \"drop\"\r\nbackend = \"ssh\"\r\nkey = \"ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAIGj+J6N6SO+4P8dOZqfR1oiay2yxhhHnagH52avUqw5h\"\r\n\r\n[git]\r\nsign-on-push = true\r\n```\r\n\r\n## Commit Signature Verification\r\n\r\nBy default signature verification and display is **disabled** as it incurs a\r\nperformance cost when rendering medium to large change logs. You can enable it\r\nby setting `ui.show-cryptographic-signatures` to true in your configuration.\r\n\r\nIf you want to display commit signatures in your templates, you can use\r\n`commit.signature()` (see [Commit type](./templates.md#commit-type)). The\r\nreturned [CryptographicSignature\r\nType](./templates.md#cryptographicsignature-type) provides methods to retrieve\r\nsignature details.\r\n\r\n## Git settings\r\n\r\n### Default colocation\r\n\r\nWhen creating a Git-backed Jujutsu repository, they use \"colocated\" mode by\r\ndefault, which places the `.git` directory next to the `.jj` directory.\r\nColocation allows some amount of two-way interoperability, but it can perform\r\nworse in large repos.\r\n\r\nThe setting `git.colocate` is a boolean option that controls whether or not the\r\n`jj git init` and `jj git clone` commands should create colocated workspace by\r\ndefault. Set `git.colocate` to `false` to disable it.\r\n\r\nSee [Colocated Jujutsu/Git workspaces](git-compatibility.md#colocated-jujutsugit-repos)\r\nfor more information.\r\n\r\n### Default remotes for `jj git fetch` and `jj git push`\r\n\r\nBy default, if a single remote exists it is used for `jj git fetch` and `jj git\r\npush`; however if multiple remotes exist, the default remote is assumed to be\r\nnamed `\"origin\"`, just like in Git. Sometimes this is undesirable, e.g. when you\r\nwant to fetch from a different remote than you push to, such as a GitHub fork.\r\n\r\nTo change this behavior, you can modify the [repository\r\nconfiguration](#config-files-and-toml) variable `git.fetch`, which can be a\r\nsingle remote, or a list of remotes to fetch from multiple places:\r\n\r\n```sh\r\njj config set --repo git.fetch \"upstream\"\r\njj config set --repo git.fetch '[\"origin\", \"upstream\"]'\r\n```\r\n\r\nBy default, the specified pattern matches remote names with glob syntax. You can\r\nalso use other [string pattern syntax](revsets.md#string-patterns):\r\n\r\n```sh\r\njj config se
